task_completion.description: Check if the previously 'Checked Out' items have been marked as 'Available'.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In column F (Status), row 6 (Victorian Jacket), row 9 (Medieval Tunic), and row 12 (Top Hat) should now display 'Available'.

task_completion.description: Check if specific costumes have been flagged as damaged.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In column G (Condition), row 6 (Victorian Jacket) and row 9 (Medieval Tunic) should be marked as 'Damaged'.

task_completion.description: Check if repair notes were added for the damaged costumes.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In column H (Notes), row 6 and row 9 should contain text describing damage or repair needs (e.g., 'torn', 'stain', 'repair', 'clean').

task_completion.description: Check if conditional formatting is applied to highlight items needing attention.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The cells containing 'Damaged' in column G (rows 6 and 9) should be visually highlighted (e.g., distinct background color like red or yellow, or bold/colored text) compared to the 'Good' cells.

integrity.description: Verify the agent used LibreOffice Calc to perform the updates.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots should show the LibreOffice Calc interface open with the costume_inventory.xlsx file, rather than a terminal or text editor.

integrity.description: Verify that other inventory items were not unintentionally modified.
integrity.visual_evidence: Rows 2 (Renaissance Gown), 4 (Flapper Dress), and 13 (Police Uniform) should remain unchanged with their original data.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The inventory has 12 items (rows 2-13). Initially, only 3 items are 'Checked Out': Victorian Jacket (row 6), Medieval Tunic (row 9), and Top Hat (row 12). These must be updated to 'Available' in column F. The Victorian Jacket (row 6) and Medieval Tunic (row 9) are the items that must be marked as 'Damaged' in column G, with corresponding repair notes in column H. Conditional formatting must visually highlight the 'Damaged' cells in column G.
